title: Everyday Slang
description: The casual, current slang young Japanese people actually say with friends every day.
situation: Hanging out with friends - reacting, joking, and chatting in relaxed, real-world Japanese.

phrases.tip: Hugely common all-purpose reaction. Originally meant 'dangerous', now it works for both amazing and terrible - tone tells you which.
Intensifier you put before anything: めっちゃ美味しい (meccha oishii) = super tasty. Originally Kansai slang, now used everywhere.
Use マジで? as a question to mean 'are you serious?'. Drop the で for a quicker マジ?
Literally 'it lands / it gets a reaction'. Say it when something is funny, like a spoken laugh.
Covers both physical tiredness and a task feeling like a hassle. Very casual, only with friends.
Widely acceptable everywhere. Friends often shorten it to すげー (sugee) for a rougher, more excited feel.
Literally 'impossible'. On its own it means 'I can't deal with this' - a go-to reaction to something overwhelming.
Casual 'wait up'. Friends often just say 待って (matte). Add the polite ください for strangers.
遊ぶ (asobu) literally means 'to play' but for adults it just means hanging out or going out together.
Literally 'money shortage'. Casual way to say you're out of cash this month.
A relaxed, cozy vibe. 家でまったりする (ie de mattari suru) = chill at home doing nothing.
From sumo slang for a genuine bout. ガチで美味しい = genuinely, no-joke delicious. A stronger マジで.
Trendy among younger speakers for something extreme in a good or bad way - similar energy to やばい.
The classic filler word. Sprinkle it in to soften statements, like English 'like'. Easy to overuse.
Drop it after a long day. Drag it out to 疲れたー (tsukareta-) for extra drama.
Casual 'let's get going'. The hyped-up English borrow レッツゴー (rettsu go-) is also common with friends.
